scenes.narration: Quantum measurement is an active process, not passive observation, fundamentally altering the system. Before measurement, quantum systems exist in a superposition of multiple states simultaneously.

Watch a qubit, represented on a Bloch sphere, existing as a blend of |0⟩ and |1⟩ states. When a measurement interaction occurs, its state instantly collapses to either the definite |0⟩ (north pole) or |1⟩ (south pole).

Observe multiple identical qubits, each in the same superposition, being measured one after another. Each measurement yields a definite but probabilistic outcome (|0⟩ or |1⟩), demonstrating the Born Rule and the irreversible nature of state collapse.

Quantum measurement is an active, irreversible process that forces a system from a probabilistic superposition into a single, definite classical state, fundamentally altering its reality and providing the final classical bit for quantum computations.
